main_summary.summary: Aaron Dixon, founding captain of the Black Panther Party's Seattle chapter, recorded this interview in Seattle, Washington, on May 11, 2013, with interviewer David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project. Dixon traces his family's migration from Mississippi and Kentucky to Chicago and then to Seattle's Madrona neighborhood in 1957-58, his politicization through marching with Martin Luther King Jr. as a teenager and hearing Stokely Carmichael speak in Seattle, and the founding of the University of Washington Black Student Union. Following the April 4, 1968 assassination of King while Dixon was jailed for the Franklin High School demonstration, he and his brother Elmer met Bobby Seale at Little Bobby Hutton's funeral in Oakland and established the Seattle Black Panther Party chapter, the first BPP office outside California. Dixon details the chapter's armed confrontations with Seattle police, the breakfast for school children program at Madrona Presbyterian Church, the first sickle-cell anemia mass testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary, Mayor Wes Uhlman's refusal to allow an ATF raid, his 1972 relocation to Oakland as bodyguard to Elaine Brown during the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign and Lionel Wilson's successful mayoral run, and the party's decline under Huey Newton's return.
key_themes: Black Panther Party Seattle chapter founding and operations
BPP community survival programs (breakfast, medical clinics, food bank, sickle-cell testing)
FBI COINTELPRO and assassination attempts on chapter leadership
BPP entry into electoral politics and Oakland municipal power
main_summary.historical_significance:
Dixon's testimony documents the BPP's national expansion from a Bay Area organization into a coast-to-coast network, with Seattle as the first chapter outside California. His first-person account establishes specific operational details rarely captured elsewhere: the Bobby Seale-led founding meeting at his parents' Seattle home, the two-weapons-and-two-thousand-rounds membership threshold, the 25-book required reading list, the 1968 Rainier Beach High School armed intervention, and Mayor Wes Uhlman's blocking of the ATF raid that had destroyed the Chicago and Los Angeles chapters.

The interview also provides a participant's view of internal BPP dynamics often obscured in published accounts: the 1969 purges, the East Coast/West Coast Newton-Cleaver split, Elaine Brown's tenure as chairperson, the party's transition from armed cadre to electoral machine that elected Oakland's first Black mayor Lionel Wilson, and Newton's post-exile destabilization of the organization through cocaine and extortion of after-hours clubs. Dixon's candor about the criminal involvement, embezzlement conviction, and FBI fugitive year that followed his BPP departure adds primary-source detail to the post-BPP biographies of rank-and-file leadership.

chapters.title: Family History and Migration
chapters.summary: Dixon traces his maternal lineage from Mississippi through his great-great-grandmother Emma, who worked in the laundry under Jim Crow, and his paternal lineage from Kentucky, where ancestors served in the Civil War on the Union side and founded the town of Brooksville. Both families participated in the Great Migration to Chicago. His father became a technical illustrator after graduating from the Art Institute.

chapters.title: Father's Military Service
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts his father Elmer Dixon's drafted Army service during World War II, including discriminatory treatment at a Mississippi base, the burning of a racist farmer's barn, witnessing a Marine mutilating a dead Japanese woman in Okinawa, and going AWOL. His father's mother wrote Eleanor Roosevelt about conditions. Post-war, Elmer Dixon became involved with Paul Robeson's youth brigade and flirted with the Communist Party.

chapters.title: Growing Up in Madrona
chapters.summary: The Dixon family relocated to Seattle in 1957-58, settling in the Madrona neighborhood after his father took a Boeing job. Dixon describes a multiracial neighborhood of Black, Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, and remaining white residents, his mother facing employment discrimination at Frederick & Nelson, his father's folk-dancing group and exposure to opera, classical music, jazz, Shakespeare, and Paul Robeson recordings, and the family expectation that all four children play instruments.

chapters.title: Political Awakening
chapters.summary: Dixon describes early exposure to racism through oral family histories, his parents' refusal to let him become a policeman at 13 or enlist for Vietnam at 16, marching with Martin Luther King Jr. in Seattle at around 12-13, demonstrating against George Wallace, and a transformative 1967 visit by Stokely Carmichael that introduced him to Black Power. He details quitting his job after a supervisor told him to remove his Ray-Bans.

chapters.title: BSU and Franklin High School
chapters.summary: Dixon enters the University of Washington through an Urban League program founded by Mrs. Richmond, helps found the first Black Student Union there with Ron Dellums and San Francisco State organizers visiting, and supports BSU formation at Garfield High School. The chapter culminates in the Franklin High School demonstration over a racially disparate disciplinary incident, leading to his arrest on April 4, 1968, the day Walter Cronkite announced King's assassination.

chapters.title: Founding the Seattle Chapter
chapters.summary: Dixon describes attending the Black Student Union conference in San Francisco, attending Little Bobby Hutton's funeral with Marlon Brando present, meeting Bobby Seale, and requesting a Seattle BPP chapter. Bobby Seale and George Murray traveled to Seattle, met with 20 prospective members at the Dixon family home, and Dixon was named first captain of what became the first BPP office outside California. He recounts being summoned to Oakland and a near-shootout at Seventh and Wood with Robert Bay, Landon and Randy Williams.

chapters.title: Armed Confrontation Summer 1968
chapters.summary: Dixon details the Seattle chapter's first year: 300 membership applications, community response calls for domestic violence and landlord disputes, the Rainier Beach High School armed intervention against white-student attacks, a brief return from a U.N. delegation trip with Bobby Seale that ended SNCC-BPP coalition after a confrontation with James Forman, and Dixon's July 1968 arrest on a setup typewriter charge that triggered Seattle's first major riot. The summer became a sustained fire-bombing and sniping campaign with Madrona Hill nicknamed Pork Chop Hill after the Korean battle.

chapters.title: First Casualties and FBI Targeting
chapters.summary: Dixon describes the first Seattle BPP casualty, a 17-year-old killed by Seattle police while defending his mother, a December 1968 casualty during a directed grocery store robbery, and his typewriter-charge acquittal when the secret witness failed to appear. J. Edgar Hoover targeted Chicago, Los Angeles, and Seattle chapters for destruction; Bunchy Carter and John Huggins were assassinated in January 1969, Fred Hampton in December 1969. A Black Justice Department official tipped off the Seattle chapter, who fortified their office with sandbags. Mayor Wes Uhlman refused the ATF's planned Seattle raid.

chapters.title: Survival Programs
chapters.summary: Dixon documents the Seattle chapter's pivot to community survival programs: five breakfast for children program locations starting at Madrona Presbyterian Church, the first free medical clinic, the first food bank program in Seattle, a free legal aid program, the bussing-to-prisons program with a donated 35-passenger bus, the first mass sickle-cell anemia testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary, and chapter funding through newspaper sales, hustled donations from Japanese-Americans tied to internment memory, and wealthy liberal contributions.

chapters.title: Assassination Attempt and Party Split
chapters.summary: Dixon recounts a 1971 shotgun explosion that nearly killed him when the gunpowder had been replaced with high explosives, his return to work within three days with a cast, Huey Newton's release from prison and his subsequent Algeria-mediated split with Eldridge Cleaver fostered by FBI letters, the New York Panthers' alignment with the Weather Underground, Huey's expulsion of the East Coast chapters, and the resulting street violence. Dixon's brother Elmer led a campaign that won a Washington gubernatorial pardon after Elmer's Oregon prison sentence.

chapters.title: Oakland Electoral Campaign
chapters.summary: Dixon relocated to Oakland for the centralization campaign, working in the Bobby Seale for Mayor of Oakland and Elaine Brown for City Council races. Five campaign offices distributed 10,000 grocery bags with a chicken in each at the Oakland Auditorium kickoff. They lost but built a political machine. Subsequent support for Jerry Brown's gubernatorial campaign yielded six judgeship appointments and ultimately the election of Lionel Wilson as Oakland's first Black mayor. Dixon discusses women's prominent BPP leadership, naming Ericka Huggins, Audrea Jones, Elaine Brown, Kathleen Cleaver, and Bertha Knox Alexander.

chapters.title: Elaine Brown's Tenure and Newton's Return
chapters.summary: Dixon details the Elaine Brown chairpersonship years: the Oakland Community School ranked among the country's best private schools, a martial arts program featured on Black Belt Magazine's cover, the first Bay Area gang intervention program funded by a CETA Grant, a seniors-against-fearful-environment program, the Lamp Post restaurant, music promotion bringing Marvin Gaye and Chaka Khan to Oakland, and Brown's Oakland Redevelopment Corporation presidency. Brown was forced out by Newton after his return from Cuban exile; Newton restarted cocaine use, extorted after-hours clubs, ordered the killing of a trial witness that went wrong, killing a Panther instead.

chapters.title: Departure and Post-BPP Life
chapters.summary: Dixon describes leaving the BPP after a confrontation with Bill Cosby over Newton's behavior, being warned that Newton planned to have him killed, his subsequent year selling cocaine and committing embezzlement that earned $100,000 and a year on the run from the FBI, conviction and approximately one year incarcerated, work as a substance-abuse counselor at the West Oakland Health Center until walking off the job after a confrontation with the executive director, time in Northern California on a marijuana farm, witnessing the crack cocaine epidemic devastate Oakland.

chapters.title: Reflections and Legacy
chapters.summary: Dixon discusses his return to Seattle, founding a nonprofit ten years prior including transition housing for 18-24 year-olds, a youth leadership project across five high schools, the first media literacy program in a Seattle high school, and his September 2012 book release. He attributes contemporary worsening of police brutality and community conditions to loss of working-class unity, Reagan-era neoliberalism and deregulation, the Reagan-Bush cocaine pipeline into Black communities, mass incarceration of 2.5 million Americans (60 percent Black and Latino men), and breakdown of family and cultural value systems.

loc_video.caption: Aaron Dixon oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Seattle, Washington, 2013 May 11
